coredata.dc:title: Luminescent determination of propineb fungicide by using a carbon quantum dots-europium ions system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiz-Medina, A.
Jiménez-López, J.
Llorent-Martínez, E.J.
coredata.dc:description: We propose a modification of lanthanide-sensitized luminescence (LSL) to increase the selectivity and sensitivity of analytical methods based on this detection. LSL consists in the formation of complexes of lanthanide ions and organic compounds. Then, an intramolecular energy transfer occurs from the excited state of the ligand (organic analyte) to the emitting level of the lanthanide. The utilization of luminescent nanoparticles (carbon quantum dots, CQDs) in LSL systems can enhance their sensitivity and selectivity. CQDs can react with lanthanide ions through their carboxylic groups. These systems can thus be used as time-resolved luminescent probes. Propineb (PPN), a well-known dithiocarbamate fungicide, has been selected as the target analyte to show the advantages of using CQDs in LSL systems. The method proposed is based on the quenching produced by PPN in europium-CQDs luminescence, obtaining a detection limit of 0.03 μg mL−1 PPN and a method detection limit of 3 mg kg−1 in capers (bud and fruit), fulfilling the maximum residue limit in these samples (25 mg kg−1). The results showed that the use of nanoparticles in LSL systems may provide novel and simple analytical methods for the screening of contaminants in the agri-food sector.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide-sensitized luminescence
Europium
Luminescent probe
Propineb
Quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of pH value and comparison of signals with different buffer solutions; 0.8 μg mL−1 PPN.
Optimization of europium and carbon quantum dots concentrations in the Eu(III)-CQDs system; 1.5 μg mL−1 PPN.
Evaluation of incubation time using 0.7 μg mL−1 PPN.
Comparison of analytical parameters in different methods for the analysis of PPN in foods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DLLME: Dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction; d-SPE: dispersive solid phase extraction; GC-FPD: gas chromatography with flame photometric detection; DBD-MES: dielectric barrier discharge-carbon molecular emission spectrometry; QL = quantification limit.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery study of PPN in caper samples.

head.ce:title: Luminescent determination of propineb fungicide by using a carbon quantum dots-europium ions system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical and Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Experimental Sciences, University of Jaén, Campus Las Lagunillas, E-23071, Jaén, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical and Analytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physical and Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Experimental Sciences, University of Jaen, Campus Las Lagunillas, E-23071, Jaen, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We propose a modification of lanthanide-sensitized luminescence (LSL) to increase the selectivity and sensitivity of analytical methods based on this detection. LSL consists in the formation of complexes of lanthanide ions and organic compounds. Then, an intramolecular energy transfer occurs from the excited state of the ligand (organic analyte) to the emitting level of the lanthanide. The utilization of luminescent nanoparticles (carbon quantum dots, CQDs) in LSL systems can enhance their sensitivity and selectivity. CQDs can react with lanthanide ions through their carboxylic groups. These systems can thus be used as time-resolved luminescent probes. Propineb (PPN), a well-known dithiocarbamate fungicide, has been selected as the target analyte to show the advantages of using CQDs in LSL systems. The method proposed is based on the quenching produced by PPN in europium-CQDs luminescence, obtaining a detection limit of 0.03 μg mL−1 PPN and a method detection limit of 3 mg kg−1 in capers (bud and fruit), fulfilling the maximum residue limit in these samples (25 mg kg−1). The results showed that the use of nanoparticles in LSL systems may provide novel and simple analytical methods for the screening of contaminants in the agri-food sector.

ce:section.ce:para: Lanthanide-sensitized luminescence (LSL) has been widely used in analytical chemistry due to its advantages as detection technique. The most common lanthanide ions used in LSL systems are Eu(III) and Tb(III) due to the following spectroscopic properties: (a) large energy gap between excited and ground states; (b) high Stokes shift; and (c) excited states of long lifetimes [1]. LSL is a luminiscent process based on the complexation of Ln (III) ions and different substances as sensitizers for their identification or quantification [2]. This process is composed of three stages. The first one is the energy absorption by the ligand, with excitation to the singlet state; the second is the intramolecular transition from the singlet state to the excited triplet state; and finally, the energy transfer from the triplet state of the ligand to the excited state of the lanthanide, which emits the luminescence signal [3].

The use of surfactants and synergistic agents is usually needed to enhance LSL sensitivity [4,5]. Another option to boost the luminescence of lanthanide complexes is the use of efficient antenna ligands, increasing the quantum efficiency [6]. Although these options are interesting, they present some handicaps. Surfactants, for example, are pollutants, whereas many of the efficient antennas reported are synthesized directly in the laboratory; hence, the lack of commercial standards may difficult their widespread use. Another alternative to improve LSL systems is to utilize nanoparticles, due to the possibility of tailoring band gap and thus energy transfer efficiency through size control, and the possible functionalization of the nanoparticle surface for specific applications [7–9]. Following this methodology, silver nanoparticles [10], gold nanoparticles [11], or graphene quantum dots [12] have been used. Here, we propose a novel approach for LSL based on the use of commercial carbon quantum dots (CQDs). These nanomaterials have gained widespread attention recently in chemical sensors, bioimaging and nanomedicine. They are monodisperse spherical nanoparticles with a carbon skeleton and a large amount of oxygen-containing groups on the surface [13]. CQDs have excellent physical, chemical and photoluminescent properties. Their size and surface chemical groups can be adjusted to produce fluorescence [14]. Different authors have reported the synthesis of Eu(III)-CQDs hybrid composites by hydrothermal method for light modulation [15], ratiometric pH sensing [16], and two-targets bioimaging [17]. However, to our knowledge, the use of Eu(III)-CQDs complexes in aqueous solution as luminiscent probes has not been reported yet.

We have selected propineb (PPN) as a model analyte to show the advantages of using CQDs in LSL with analytical purposes. PPN [polymeric zinc propylene-bis-(dithiocarbamate)], [(C5H8N2S4Zn)x], is a polymeric dithiocarbamate fungicide. Dithiocarbamates are a group of organosulfur fungicides with broad-spectrum activity against various plant pathogens; they have low acute mammal toxicity, and the production costs are low. Thus, dithiocarbamates are widely used in agriculture combined with other modern systemic fungicides [18]. Specifically, PPN is biotransformed to propylene thiourea, carbon disulfide, and releases zinc. The two last are responsible for its neurotoxicity [19]. Different methods have been proposed for PPN determination, such as liquid chromatography [20,21], gas chromatography [22], electrochemical methods [23,24], and spectroscopic methods [25,26].

Here, we report the use of CQDs nanoparticles to improve the analytical characteristics of LSL detection, using europium ions as the lanthanide. The approach proposed consists of the quenching effect produced by PPN on Eu(III)-CQDs luminescence. Both sensitivity and selectivity were improved by the additional use of CQDs in the LSL system, demonstrating the potential of Eu(III) and CQDs combination as luminescent probes for quantitative purposes.
Luminescence measurements were recorded with a Cary Eclipse luminescence spectrometer (Varian, Mulgrave, Australia) with a Hellma 1010-QS cell.

A high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using a FEI Titan (FEI Europe B·V., Eindhoven, Netherlands) at 300 kV was used for the study of the morphology and microstructure of CQDs (Fig. 1 ). The samples for HRTEM were made by depositing an aqueous solution onto a Cu carbon film. It allowed 500× magnification range from up to 400000×. The used HRTEM has an Image CS corrected and digital images were captured by using a camera Gatan model GIF2002P.

Sodium hydroxide (≥99%), sodium acetate 3-hydrate (≥99%), trishidroxymethylaminomethane (99.5%), hydrochloric acid (37%) were bought from Panreac (Barcelona, Spain). CQDs, acetonitrile (99.8%), sodium borohydride (98%), sodium phosphate dibasic (99.9%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (≥99%), graphitized carbon black (GCB), Eu(III) nitrate pentahydrate (99.9%), and interferents were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (Madrid, Spain). Isolute QuEChERS kits were acquired from Biotage (Sweden).
Ultrapure water (Milli-Q water purification system) was utilized in all experiments.

PPN (≥%) was purchased from Dr. Ehrenstorfer (Augsburg, Germany). A stock solution of 20 mg L−1 was prepared in water. Stock solutions of Eu (III) (0.02 mol L−1) and CQDs (100 mg L−1) were prepared in water. Suitable dilutions of these reagents were also made in ultrapure water.

All solutions were prepared in 2-mL centrifuge tubes, completing to volume with suitable amounts of water. The procedure was the following: required volumes of stock solutions of Eu(III), CQDs, and PPN were added to the tubes to obtain concentrations of 2.5 × 10−3 mol L−1 Eu(III), 10 mg L−1 CQDs and 0.1–2 μg mL−1 PPN. After shaking for 10 s, the solution was transferred to a 1-cm Hellma quartz cell and the emission spectrum was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 388 nm. The following conditions were used: excitation/emission slit widths of 20/20 nm/nm, delay time of 0.1 ms, gate time of 3 ms, and photomultiplier tube voltage of 900 V.

Peak heights at 618 nm were measured for all solutions. The analytical signal was then calculated as net analytical signal: signal of Eu(III)-CQDs minus signal of Eu(III)-CQDs-PPN; that is, the analytical signal used to construct the calibration graph was the quenching produced by PPN in the original Eu(III)-CQDs signal.
Two different trademarks of capers (bud and fruit) were bought at local markets. 50 g of each sample was ground and homogenized. A QuEChERS extraction adapted from Ref. [27] was used.

A QuEChERS extraction kit with GCB was used. GCB was used due to the high pigmentation of greenish samples; this non-polar sorbent removes hydrophobic compounds such as chlorophylls, present in this type of matrices.

10 g of sample and 10 mL acetonitrile were mixed in a 50 mL PTFE centrifuge tube. The content of a 15-mL tube extraction kit (4 g MgSO4, 1 g Na3C6H5O7, 0.5 g Na2C6H6O7 · 1.5H2O and 1 g NaCl) was added, and the tube was vortexed for 1 min. After centrifugation (5 min, 4000 rpm),6 mL of the upper layer was transferred into a 15 mL dispersive SPE tube with the sorbent (150 mg PSA and 900 mg MgSO4) and 25 mg of GCB. Samples were vortexed again, centrifuged (5 min, 4000 rpm) and an appropriate volume of the supernatant was diluted with ultrapure water before analysis. It was not required to adjust the pH value.
Recovery yields around 80–85% were obtained within the use of GCB, versus recovery yields around 100% by using suitable amounts of GCB in the clean-up procedure.

Before proposing the Eu(III)-CQDs system for the determination of PPN, different experiments were performed to ensure that this system would provide the best luminescence signal. Firstly, luminescence spectra of CQDs were recorded, obtaining maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 345 and 445 nm (fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). Secondly, solutions of CQDs + PPN were also prepared and their luminescence spectra were recorded to check potential interactions. However, PPN did not produce any change in the luminescence signals, so CQDs could not be used on their own as luminescence probes for PPN. Therefore, the potential use of LSL for the quantification of PPN was tested.

We observed that PPN decreased the luminescence of Ln (III), using Tb(III) or Eu(III) (Figs. S2 and S3, Supplementary Material). However, the best sensitivity was obtained using Eu(III), which was consequently selected as the lanthanide ion. We thus tested the effect of adding CQDs to the solutions, observing approximately a 40% increase in sensitivity. Hence, Eu(III)-CQDs was selected as luminescent probe for further experiments. Excitation and emission spectra of his system are given in Fig. S4, Supplementary Material.

The Stern-Volmer plot was constructed (Fig. S5, Supplementary Material), observing a linear relationship between I0/I and [Q], where I0 is the luminescence signal in the absence of quencher, I is the signal in the presence of quencher, and [Q] is quencher concentration (PPN concentration). The Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) was 0.1344 μg mL−1 (R = 0.9991). Therefore, the plausible mechanism is a collisional quenching.

Eu(III)-CQDs interaction could be based on the formation of a luminescent complex through the carboxylic groups of CQDs. In lanthanide-based luminescent systems, the use of surfactants and synergetic agents (mainly to exclude water, which is a potential quencher) are used. However, different approaches can be used to avoid the use of these compounds, therefore providing more environmentally-friendly analytical methods. As an example, using a solid-support to retain lanthanide complexes proved successful to stabilize terbium complexes [2]. In this work, CQDs provides additional stability to europium and probably act as water-exclusion ligands, therefore improving the analytical signal (higher PPN quenching). An advantage of the selected nanoparticles is their commercial availability, which makes them easy to use in different laboratories.

Excitation and emission spectra of Eu(III)-CQDs system were recorded, selecting 388 and 618 nm as optimum wavelengths. Both wavelengths corresponded to europium (Fig. S4, Supplementary Material). The emission wavelength corresponded to the Eu(III) 5D0→7F2 transition, which is the most sensitive of Eu(III) emission bands along with 5D0→7F1 (592 nm). Although the highest signal for Eu(III) was recorded at 592 nm, the band at 618 nm suffered the highest quenching from PPN. Then, the delay time (0.1–0.3 ms) and the gate time (1–5 ms) were optimized, selecting times of 0.1 and 3 ms as optimum, respectively.

Finally, we optimized the excitation and emission slit widths (5–20 nm) and the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (500–1000 V). The best sensitivity and widest linear dynamic range were obtained using 20 nm for both excitation and emission slit widths and a photomultiplier tube voltage of 900 V (highest blank signal (Eu(III)-CQDs signal)).
The performance of the method was strongly influenced by the pH of the solution, the concentration of lanthanide and nanoparticles, and the incubation time.

We optimized the pH value in the range 3–12 (Fig. 2 ). For acidic pH values, the analytical signal decreased due to the protonation of the carboxylic groups present in CQDs, which prevented their interaction with Eu (III). For pH values higher than 8, the hydrolysis of europium occurs, so the analytical signal also diminished. Hence, pH values between 6 and 7 produced the best analytical signal. The use of buffers (Tris-HCl and phosphate) was tested, but decreases in the analytical signal were observed (Fig. 2). Therefore, no buffers were used, as the high dilution (approximately 100-fold) with water after the QuEChERS treatment resulted in pH values of 6–7. Hence, no additional reagents were required, simplifying the method and minimizing wastes generation.

We further investigated the effect of Eu(III) and CQDs concentrations on the luminescence intensity (Fig. 3 ). Firstly, we optimized Eu(III) concentration in the absence of CQDs, selecting 2.5 × 10−3 M Eu(III) as optimum. Then, using this Eu(III) concentration, CQDs concentration was optimized, selecting a value of 10 mg L−1 CQDS, which provided an enhancement of the analytical signal of approximately 40% compared with the absence of CQDs.
Finally, the study of incubation time was performed. As can be seen in Fig. 4 , the analytical signal decreased over time. Therefore, all solutions were measured immediately after preparation.

The effects of some potentially interfering substances that may coexist in these matrices were investigated to evaluate the developed analytical method. The main compounds that may interfere in the PPN determination in food samples are other contaminants, such as pesticides. We assumed that no interference took place if the variation of the signal was lower than 4%.

Tolerance levels were 20 for carbendazim, chlorpyrifos, nitenpyram, dinotefuran, azoxystrobin, and spinosad; 10 for pyraclostrobin, acetamiprid, λ-cihalotrin, deltamethrin, and glyphosate; and 5 for thiacloprid, thiamethoxam, clothianidin, imidacloprid. Hence, good tolerances were obtained for all the tested compounds, which included some pesticides commonly used in caper crops, such as glyphosate and azoxystrobin [28].

We compared the results obtained with the use of merely Eu(III) and Eu(III)-CQDs complexes (using the same PPN concentration). The addition of CQDs produced an increase in selectivity for most of the potential interferences, specifically, a 100% increase in the tolerance ratios. Only in the case of nitenpyram, dinotefuran, thiamethoxam, and imidacloprid, this increase was 60%. These results indicated that the addition of CQDs improved both sensitivity and selectivity, which may expand the range of applications of LSL systems.
The analytical parameters were carefully examined under the optimized conditions, and a summary can be shown in Table 1 .

Calibration curves were constructed by representing net analytical signal (quenching produced by PPN) vs concentration. The developed analytical method presented a good linear relationship between the quenched luminescence intensity and the concentration of PPN over the range of 0.1–2 μg mL−1. Detection and quantitation limits were calculated as the concentration of PPN that produced an analytical signal equal to three and ten times, respectively, the standard deviation of the background luminescence, obtaining a detection limit of 0.03 μg mL−1 and a quantification limit of 0.1 μg mL−1. This detection limit implies an approximately 40% improvement compared to the use of Eu(III) with no CQDs addition. The method detection and quantitation limits (including sample treatment and required dilution) were also calculated in real samples (capers: buds and fruits), obtaining values of 3 and 10 mg kg−1 PPN, respectively.

A comparison between the proposed method and previous methods for the analysis of PPN in food samples is shown in Table 2 . The proposed method presents lower sensitivity than most chromatographic methods. However, the quantification limit is low enough to fulfill the Maximum Residue Limit (25 mg kg−1). In terms of sample treatment, the QuEChERS method has been also used in other works and it is simpler than other proposed sample treatments. Our method also presents recoveries and RSD similar or better than chromatographic methods. Therefore, although it presents some limitations, such as the unavailability to analyze more than one analyte, it represents an easy-to use alternative to chromatographic methods.

Repeatability was established for ten independent analyses of solutions with 0.2 and 0.8 μg mL−1 of PPN, whereas intermediate precision was calculated for ten consecutive days, obtaining Relative Standard Deviations (RSD) lower or equal to 5% in all cases. The robustness of the system was studied by varying some conditions (±5% variations in excitation and emission wavelengths, photomultiplier tube voltage, and Eu(III) and CQDs concentrations), observing variations lower than 4% in all cases.

We selected samples of capers (buds and fruits) to demonstrate the applicability of the method for the analysis of PPN in real samples. All samples were purchased at local supermarkets, two commercial brands of each type of caper. Recovery experiments were performed to evaluate the accuracy of the method.

Firstly, an initial analysis of samples was performed to confirm the absence of PPN in the samples. Secondly, potential matrix effects were evaluated by preparing PPN standard solutions in caper extracts blanks by external calibration and standard addition methodology. As no matrix effect was observed, we selected external calibration for further experiments.

Caper samples were spiked with appropriate PPN concentrations, at levels close to the established maximum residue limit (MRL) of PPN in caper samples, 25 mg kg−1 [33]. Then, spiked samples were thoroughly homogenized and submitted to sample treatment. The results are summarized in Table 3 , observing recovery yields between 95% and 104% and RSDs lower than 5%.

Finally, we also used the method of the average recovery [34]. The mean recovery was calculated from the individual recoveries collected in Table 3. This value was then tested for significance by the Student t-test. An experimental t value of 1.558 (n = 12) was obtained, whereas the tabulated value for n-1° of freedom and a 95% confidence level is 1.796. Hence, we can ensure the accuracy of the proposed method.

For the first time, a luminescent strategy based on the quenching effect of PPN over the luminescence of CQDs-Eu(III) complex has been reported. PPN quenched the analytical signal of this complex, proportionally to PPN concentration. Although PPN could quench Eu(III) signal, the use of CQDs provided additional selectivity and sensitivity, important aspects when analyzing food samples. Therefore, the strategy of combining LSL detection and the use of nanoparticles can expand the range of applications of luminescent probes in the agri-food sector. PPN was analyzed in real samples (caper buds and fruits) obtaining recovery yields close to 100% and RSDs lower than 5%. We could thus conclude that the proposed method may represent an alternative to other methods currently used for quality control of PPN in food matrices.

Julia Jiménez-López: Methodology, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Data Curation. Antonio Ruiz-Medina: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Eulogio J. Llorent-Martínez: Methodology, Data Curation, Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Centro de Instrumentación Científica (CICT) from the University of Granada for the availability of the HRTEM instrument.
